---
title: "FinalDataVizAD"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    code_folding: show
    self_contained: true
date: "2026-06-20"
---
# Who were you miss girl? 
Anastasiya Dmytryk Final Project Data Visualization
## An analysis of pinterest useres 18-24 years old from 2025


Pinterest is a social media platform where people share and save images. It collects a large amount of data and is a primary place where useres of all ages look for inspiration. Pinterest is also my dream company to work for.
Pinterest releases a lot of its data publicly, making it very convenient for analysis. In this project, I want to focus on a relatively small demographic women aged 18-24 and go over how their style and taste were shaped over the course of 2025.
For this analysis, we will look at the dataset I collected and enriched with an LLM for a final project in Data Wrangling, as well as some statistics taken from Pinterest's audience insight overview.

# Age Overview. 
## Making the original even better 
Firstly Pinterest provides us with an age distribution overview table on their insights website.
![Age distribution](images/AgeDist.png)
I wanted to rework it to make it more visually interesting
```{r, fig.alt="Female Pinterest users by age"}
library(ggplot2)
library(ggimage)

age_data <- data.frame(
  age_group = c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-49"),
  percent = c(30, 32, 14, 5),
  image = "~/Downloads/ChatGPT Image Jun 22, 2026, 09_33_36 PM.png"
)


age_colors <- c(
  "18-24" = "#0072B2",  # blue
  "25-34" = "#E69F00",  # orange
  "35-44" = "#009E73",  # green
  "45-49" = "#CC79A7"   # purple
)

ggplot(age_data,
       aes(x = age_group,
           y = 0.5,
           image = image,
           size = percent)) +

  geom_image() +

  scale_size_area(
    max_size = 0.45,
    guide = "none"
  ) +

  geom_text(
    aes(
      y = -0.15,
      label = paste0(age_group, "\n", percent, "%"),
      color = age_group
    ),
    size = 5,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +

  scale_color_manual(values = age_colors, guide = "none") +

  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-0.4, 1.1), clip = "off") +

  labs(
    title = "Who Uses Pinterest?",
    subtitle = "Audience distribution by age group"
  ) +

  theme_void() +

  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(
      face = "bold",
      size = 18,
      hjust = 0.5
    ),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(
      hjust = 0.5,
      size = 12
    ),
    plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 40, 20)
  )
```

From this visualization we see that users with the most accounts are age ranged 25-34, however the age group that this research is focused on is 18-24 which is only 2% behind. it is still more than 1/4th of all Pinterest User population and has specific interest to me a s a researcher since I can be an expert on the subject as I'm part of the group.

```{r setup, include=FALSE}

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(plotly)
library(lubridate)
df <- read.csv(
  "~/Downloads/ai-trend-forecast/data/normalized_enriched_trends.csv",
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

female_18_24 <- df %>%
  filter(age_group == "18-24", gender == "female") %>%
  filter(!is.na(search_volume)) %>%
  mutate(
    week  = as.Date(week),
    month = floor_date(week, unit = "month")
  )



```
# Lets get geographical.
Pinterest provides a breakdown of the top percentage of users in the United States. The data is portrayed as a vertical bar graph.

![pinterest chart](images/Location.png)
# Put her on a map 
After collecting the data from the Pinterest website, I decided to improve on that graphic by plotting it on an actual map of the United States.
Because I'm only looking at a small demographic, I accounted for how many Pinterest users overall fall into that age group, then derived the percentage of 18-24 users specifically for each metro area.
In this map, the size of each dot shows the estimated share of female Pinterest users aged 18-24 in that U.S. metro area.


```{r, fig.alt="Map of the United States with dot size showing estimated share of female Pinterest users aged 18-24 by metro area"}
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)
library(maps)

us_map <- map_data("state")

metro_data <- tribble(
  ~metro, ~pct_audience, ~lat, ~lon,
  "New York, NY", 0.055, 40.7128, -74.0060,
  "Los Angeles, CA", 0.046, 34.0522, -118.2437,
  "Chicago, IL", 0.025, 41.8781, -87.6298,
  "Dallas, TX", 0.023, 32.7767, -96.7970,
  "Washington, DC", 0.022, 38.9072, -77.0369,
  "Atlanta, GA", 0.021, 33.7490, -84.3880,
  "Philadelphia, PA", 0.020, 39.9526, -75.1652,
  "Seattle, WA", 0.018, 47.6062, -122.3321,
  "Phoenix, AZ", 0.017, 33.4484, -112.0740,
  "San Francisco, CA", 0.016, 37.7749, -122.4194
)

ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = us_map,
    aes(long, lat, group = group),
    fill = "gray95",
    color = "white",
    linewidth = 0.2
  ) +

  geom_point(
    data = metro_data,
    aes(x = lon, y = lat, size = pct_audience),
    color = "deeppink3",
    alpha = 0.75
  ) +

  geom_text_repel(
    data = metro_data,
    aes(x = lon, y = lat, label = metro),
    size = 3
  ) +

  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(4, 20),
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 0.1),
    name = "Estimated Share\nWomen 18–24"
  ) +

  coord_fixed(1.3) +

  labs(
    title = "Estimated Distribution of Female Pinterest Users Aged 18–24",
    subtitle = "Top U.S. metro areas by audience share",
    caption = "Values are estimated from Pinterest audience breakdowns."
  ) +

  theme_void( ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold", hjust = 1),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right"
  )
```
#Maps speak louder
Compared to the bar graph made by Pinterest, we can now actually visualize our target population and see where it's spread across the country.
As expected, New York has the largest share, since it's one of the international fashion capitals. LA, San Francisco, and Chicago also make sense relative to their overall population size. What's surprising is seeing cities like Dallas, Atlanta, Phoenix, Washington D.C., Philadelphia, and Seattle on this chart, these metros hold a meaningfully larger share of this audience than their general population size alone would predict, which suggests fashion and beauty-related Pinterest activity is concentrated there for reasons beyond just city size. As a frequent Pinterest scroller and social-media girly, I hadn't noticed a big fashion scene in these areas, though I have noticed Atlanta and Dallas developing their own distinct style.
Enough about this map, let's talk about our user.
If I had to flip a coin based on this data, I'd say our girl lives in either New York or LA, since those two metros account for the largest combined share in the dataset.

Now what else can we say about her? 
# Digging in deeper 
Based on the data collected from Pinterest, we can see which searches were most popular and in our case, searches = trends, because what people search for reflects what they're seeing and engaging with. This dataset only covers topics I selected myself as a fashion girly, and since I'm within this age range, I'd say that makes me something of an expert in the field. So, as a self proclaimed expert, let's look at our four categories:
* Nails
* Fashion
* Beauty
* Makeup

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE, fig.alt="Monthly normalized interests in 2025" }
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(tibble)
library(plotly)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(packcircles)
normalized <- female_18_24 %>%
  group_by(base_keyword) %>%
  mutate(
    norm_volume = (search_volume - min(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE)) /
      (max(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE) - min(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE)) * 100
  ) %>%
  ungroup()
monthly_keyword_avg <- normalized %>%
  group_by(base_keyword, month) %>%
  summarise(avg_norm_volume = mean(norm_volume, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(base_keyword, month)

keyword_colors <- c(
  "nails"   = "#534AB7",
  "fashion" = "#D85A30",
  "beauty"  = "#0F6E56",
  "makeup"  = "#D4537E"
)
keyword_plots <- lapply(names(keyword_colors), function(kw) {
  d <- monthly_keyword_avg %>% filter(base_keyword == kw)
  plot_ly(
    data = d,
    x = ~month,
    y = ~avg_norm_volume,
    type = "scatter",
    mode = "lines+markers",
    line = list(color = keyword_colors[[kw]], width = 2),
    marker = list(color = keyword_colors[[kw]], size = 7),
    hovertemplate = paste0(
      "Month: %{x|%b %Y}<br>Normalized score: %{y:.1f}<extra></extra>"
    ),
    name = kw  # gives this trace a name, used below for annotation matching
  ) %>%
    layout(
      xaxis = list(title = "Month"),
      yaxis = list(title = "Normalized score (0-100)", range = c(0, 100))
    )
})
names(keyword_plots) <- names(keyword_colors)

keyword_grid <- subplot(
  keyword_plots[["nails"]],
  keyword_plots[["fashion"]],
  keyword_plots[["beauty"]],
  keyword_plots[["makeup"]],
  nrows = 2, shareX = FALSE, titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE, margin = 0.1
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Monthly normalized interest by keyword - female, 18-24",
    showlegend = TRUE
  )

keyword_grid
```
This graph shows an interactive view of how popular the normalized searches were for each category. It's statistically interesting, and we can draw some solid conclusions from it. Nails saw a dip in interest during the fall-winter season of 2025 which is around when donut nails were popular. Fashion peaked during the summer, with everyone out of school and leaning into the European-summer and "brat summer" trends. This peak likely reflects the freedom to express personal style without school dress codes, and unsurprisingly, once school starts back up, fashion interest drops off  kind of like winter hibernation for the fashion icons.
Beauty had a small but steady downward slope across the year; there's no obvious single cause, since there aren't any major spikes or drops to point to. Last but not least, makeup: interest in makeup stayed consistently high among young women all year, and I don't expect that to change anytime soon. Between filters and influencer culture, makeup feels less optional and more like a daily expectation for a lot of girls trying to match an idealized look they see online

# what specifically did you like each month
Before we only looked at the top topics, but pinterest has sub topics for each one of them, Lets see what was the most popular topic and subtopic per every month. 
```{r plot-timeline}
trend_year_totals <- normalized %>%
  group_by(base_keyword, Trend) %>%
  summarise(trend_year_total = sum(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

top_trend_per_month <- normalized %>%
  left_join(trend_year_totals, by = c("base_keyword", "Trend")) %>%
  arrange(month, desc(search_volume), desc(trend_year_total), Trend) %>%
  group_by(month) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(month)
top_trend_per_month <- top_trend_per_month %>%
  mutate(
    y_flat = 0,
    point_color = unname(keyword_colors[base_keyword]),
    hover_text = paste0(
      "<b>", Trend, "</b><br>",
      "Keyword: ", base_keyword, "<br>",
      "Month: ", format(month, "%b %Y"), "<br>",
      "Score: ", round(search_volume, 1), "<br>",
      "Category: ", category
    )
  )

timeline_plot <- plot_ly()

# One dummy trace per keyword, just to populate a clean legend
# (color = keyword), since the real markers are one combined trace.
for (kw in names(keyword_colors)) {
  timeline_plot <- timeline_plot %>%
    add_trace(
      x = NA, y = NA,
      type = "scatter", mode = "markers",
      marker = list(color = keyword_colors[[kw]], size = 10),
      name = kw,
      showlegend = TRUE
    )
}

timeline_plot <- timeline_plot %>%
  add_trace(
    data = top_trend_per_month,
    x = ~month,
    y = ~y_flat,
    type = "scatter",
    mode = "lines+markers",
    text = ~hover_text,
    hoverinfo = "text",
    line = list(color = "#B4B2A9", width = 2),
    marker = list(color = ~point_color, size = 16, line = list(color = "white", width = 1.5)),
    showlegend = FALSE
  ) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Monthly top trend timeline - female, 18-24",
    xaxis = list(title = "Month", showgrid = FALSE, zeroline = FALSE),
    yaxis = list(
      title = "",
      range = c(-1, 1),
      showticklabels = FALSE,
      showgrid = FALSE,
      zeroline = FALSE,
      fixedrange = TRUE
    ),
    legend = list(orientation = "h", y = -0.2, title = list(text = "Keyword"))
  )

timeline_plot
```


To see what caught the most attention each month, we look at the single most popular search per month and based on that, we can reasonably assume that if it was the most popular search, our average girl probably looked it up too. Almond nails dominate as the top topic across the most months of any single trend. We're starting to build a picture here: it's likely our girl had almond nails at some point that year. We also know she wanted to look her best around the holidays, since makeup was the top trend in both December and January.
Interesting peak: the top January trend is specifically "birthday makeup", but after some digging online We found out January is not that common to be a birthday month. 
Fenty Beauty was the top trend in November, which can be explained by the launch of their new products Holiday Glow Collection.
Also, same pattern as before: when school starts, interests shift. The monthly breakdown showed fashion trending downward once school starts, yet makeup is still the top topic in August. One theory: when she can't express herself as freely through clothing (school dress codes), she may shift that self-expression toward makeup instead.

# Have you heard of seasonal depression? 
We do it better, we do seasonal analysis. The dataset I collected is pretty messy because I used an LLM to enrich it, so now we'll clean it up.
To define the seasons accurately, we won't rely on the categories the LLM generated; instead, we'll derive them ourselves from the actual date each data point was collected. For example, December through February will be defined as winter.
```{r clean-occasion}
clean_occasion <- function(x) {
  x <- tolower(x)
  case_when(
    is.na(x)                                                    ~ "unspecified",
    grepl("none", x)                                             ~ "unspecified",
    grepl("wedding", x)                                          ~ "wedding",
    grepl("halloween", x)                                        ~ "holiday_seasonal",
    grepl("holiday|festival|valentine", x)                       ~ "holiday_seasonal",
    grepl("nightlife|party", x)                                  ~ "party_nightlife",
    grepl("formal|special|event", x)                             ~ "formal_special",
    grepl("casual|daily|everyday|leisure|street|personal", x)    ~ "casual_daily",
    grepl("evening", x)                                          ~ "evening",
    TRUE                                                          ~ "other"
  )
}
clean_category <- function(x) {
  x <- tolower(x)
  case_when(
    is.na(x)                                                              ~ "unspecified",
    grepl("nail", x)                                                      ~ "nails",
    grepl("makeup|cosmetic|skincare", x)                                  ~ "makeup_cosmetics",
    grepl("beauty", x)                                                    ~ "beauty_general",
    grepl("hair", x)                                                      ~ "hair",
    grepl("cloth|apparel|dress|footwear|outerwear|formal_wear|garment|streetwear|outfit|fashion", x) ~ "fashion_apparel",
    grepl("home|living|decor", x)                                        ~ "home_decor",
    grepl("body_art|costume|toy", x)                                     ~ "body_art_costume",
    grepl("subculture|philosophy|personal_development", x)              ~ "lifestyle_identity",
    grepl("photography|television|media", x)                            ~ "media",
    TRUE                                                                  ~ "other"
  )
}
clean_theme <- function(x) {
  x <- tolower(x)
  case_when(
    is.na(x)                                                                          ~ "unspecified",
    grepl("retro|nostalg|throwback|vintage", x)                                       ~ "retro_nostalgic",
    grepl("minimalis|clean,_functional", x)                                           ~ "minimalist",
    grepl("glam|luxury|high_end|high_fashion|elegan|chic|classic|bold_&_vibrant|dramatic", x) ~ "glam_elegant",
    grepl("edgy|dark|gothic|rebellious|alternative", x)                               ~ "edgy_dark",
    grepl("feminine|romantic|dreamy|soft|pastel|whimsical|cute|childlike|playful|bright_and_cheerful", x) ~ "soft_feminine_playful",
    grepl("fairy|fantasy|mythical|disney|comic", x)                                   ~ "fantasy_fairytale",
    grepl("k_beauty|k_pop|korean|japanese|asian", x)                                  ~ "asian_pop_culture",
    grepl("holiday|wedding|bridal|celebration|formal_event|formal|halloween", x)      ~ "celebration_event",
    grepl("summer|spring|autumn|winter|warm|blossom|sun_and_fun|seasonal", x)         ~ "seasonal_nature",
    grepl("nature|botanical|earth|animal|feline", x)                                  ~ "nature_inspired",
    grepl("urban|streetwear|hip_hop|casual", x)                                       ~ "urban_streetwear",
    grepl("skincare|self_care|wellness|routine|tips|how_to|tutorial|beauty_enhancement|beauty_and_personal_care|beauty_retail|inner_beauty|inspiration", x) ~ "routine_selfcare",
    grepl("inclusive|unconventional|modesty|cultural|middle_eastern", x)              ~ "inclusive_cultural",
    grepl("social_media|trend|travel|virtual|gaming|celebrity", x)                    ~ "trend_digital",
    grepl("nail_art|colorful_nail|creative_nail|glittery|geometric|artificial_nail|natural_shades|beauty_&_nail_care|nails_colors", x) ~ "nail_art_style",
    grepl("hair_care|hair_styling", x)                                                ~ "hair_care",
    grepl("interior|home_decor|organization|organiz", x)                              ~ "home_lifestyle",
    grepl("humorous|exclusive|unique|accessor|makeup_application|colorful_makeup|artistic_and_creative", x) ~ "misc_other",
    x == "makeup"                                                                     ~ "misc_other",
    TRUE                                                                               ~ "other"
  )
}
by_season <- normalized %>%
  mutate(
    cal_month = month(week),
    season = case_when(
      cal_month %in% c(3, 4, 5)  ~ "spring",
      cal_month %in% c(6, 7, 8)  ~ "summer",
      cal_month %in% c(9, 10, 11) ~ "fall",
      TRUE                        ~ "winter"
    )
  )
by_season <- by_season %>%
  mutate(
    occasion_clean = clean_occasion(occasion),
    category_clean = clean_category(category),
    theme_clean = clean_theme(theme)
  )
```

Lets see how our users interest were flowing through the different seasons in 2025 
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(plotly)
library(scales)

#--------------------------------------------------
# 1. Select top themes
#--------------------------------------------------

top_themes <- by_season %>%
  group_by(theme_clean) %>%
  summarise(total = mean(norm_volume, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  slice_max(total, n = 5) %>%
  pull(theme_clean)

theme_flow <- by_season %>%
  filter(theme_clean %in% top_themes) %>%
  group_by(season, theme_clean) %>%
  summarise(
    volume = mean(norm_volume, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


season_nodes <- c("Spring", "Summer", "Fall", "Winter")

theme_nodes <- sort(unique(theme_flow$theme_clean))

nodes <- data.frame(
  label = c(season_nodes, theme_nodes),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)


season_colors <- c(
  "Spring" = "#7CB342",
  "Summer" = "#F9A825",
  "Fall"   = "#A65E2E",
  "Winter" = "#42A5F5"
)

# Generate automatic colors for themes
theme_palette <- setNames(
  hcl.colors(length(theme_nodes), "Dynamic"),
  theme_nodes
)

node_colors <- c(
  season_colors,
  theme_palette
)

nodes$color <- node_colors[nodes$label]


links <- theme_flow %>%
  mutate(
    season_label = tools::toTitleCase(season),

    source = match(season_label, nodes$label) - 1,

    target = match(theme_clean, nodes$label) - 1,

    # edge color = theme color
    color = alpha(theme_palette[theme_clean], 0.55),

    hover_text = paste0(
      "<b>", season_label, "</b>",
      " → ",
      "<b>", gsub("_", " ", theme_clean), "</b>",
      "<br><br>",
      "Average Interest: ",
      round(volume, 1)
    )
  )

fig <- plot_ly(
  type = "sankey",
  arrangement = "snap",

  node = list(
    label = nodes$label,
    color = nodes$color,

    pad = 45,        # spacing between nodes
    thickness = 30,

    line = list(
      color = "black",
      width = 0.5
    ),

    hovertemplate =
      "<b>%{label}</b><extra></extra>"
  ),

  link = list(
    source = links$source,
    target = links$target,
    value  = links$volume,

    color = links$color,

    customdata = links$hover_text,

    hovertemplate =
      "%{customdata}<extra></extra>"
  )
)

fig %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "Pinterest Theme Flow Across Seasons",
      font = list(size = 22)
    ),

    font = list(size = 14),

    hoverlabel = list(
      bgcolor = "white",
      font = list(size = 14)
    )
  )
```

This interactive graph shows the 5 most popular themes for our user, broken down by season.
From the chart, we can see that in fall, she was leaning into an edgy, dark aesthetic — fitting for Halloween and the spooky themes typically associated with that season.
In summer, nail-related searches were more frequent — every girl needs fresh nails for summer vacation. The same pattern shows up in winter, when nails trend again as the holidays approach. Winter also shares fall's interest in edgy/dark aesthetics, alongside a minimalist theme. Spring brings nail art back into focus once more, with minimalist aesthetics close behind.

Now let's build an image of the charachter 
```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(htmltools)
library(stringr)
top_trends_18_24 <- df %>%
  filter(age_group == "18-24") %>%
  group_by(base_keyword, Trend) %>%
  summarise(total_volume = sum(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(base_keyword) %>%
  slice_max(total_volume, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

get_top <- function(keyword) {
  row <- top_trends_18_24 %>% filter(base_keyword == keyword)
  if (nrow(row) == 0) return("No data available")
  paste0(row$Trend[1], " (", round(row$total_volume[1]), " avg. searches)")
}
get_top_within_keyword <- function(parent_keyword, match_pattern) {
  rows <- df %>%
    filter(age_group == "18-24", base_keyword == parent_keyword) %>%
    filter(str_detect(str_to_lower(Trend), match_pattern)) %>%
    group_by(Trend) %>%
    summarise(total_volume = sum(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    slice_max(total_volume, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE)

  if (nrow(rows) == 0) return("No data available")
  paste0(rows$Trend[1], " (", round(rows$total_volume[1]), " avg. searches)")
}

top_hair    <- get_top_within_keyword("beauty", "hair")
top_beauty  <- get_top("beauty")
top_face    <- get_top("makeup")
top_nails   <- get_top("nails")
top_outfit  <- get_top("outfit")
top_fashion <- get_top("fashion")

label_hair    <- paste0("<b>Top Hair Trend:</b> ", top_hair)
label_face    <- paste0("<b>Top Makeup Trend:</b> ", top_face)
label_clothes <- paste0("<b>Top Outfit Trend:</b> ", top_outfit,
                         "<br><b>Top Fashion Trend:</b> ", top_fashion)
label_nails   <- paste0("<b>Top Nails Trend:</b> ", top_nails)

widget_html <- HTML(sprintf('
<style>
  .persona-wrap {
    position: relative;
    width: 280px;
    margin: 0 auto;
    font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif;
    text-align: center;
  }
  .persona-emoji {
    font-size: 220px;
    line-height: 1;
    user-select: none;
  }
  .hotspot {
    position: absolute;
    cursor: pointer;
    border: 2px dashed transparent;
    border-radius: 8px;
    transition: background 0.2s, border-color 0.2s;
  }
  .hotspot:hover {
    background: rgba(207, 56, 169, 0.18);
    border-color: #CF38A9;
  }
  .hotspot.active {
    background: rgba(207, 56, 169, 0.28);
    border-color: #CF38A9;
  }
  #zone-hair    { top: 0%%;   left: 22%%; width: 56%%; height: 16%%; }
  #zone-face    { top: 12%%;  left: 28%%; width: 44%%; height: 20%%; }
  #zone-clothes { top: 25%%;  left: 18%%; width: 64%%; height: 38%%; }
  #zone-nails-l { top: 40%%;  left: 10%%;  width: 16%%; height: 16%%; }
  #zone-nails-r { top: 40%%;  left: 70%%; width: 16%%; height: 16%%; }

  .persona-label {
    margin-top: 10px;
    min-height: 60px;
    padding: 12px 16px;
    border-radius: 10px;
    background: #FFF4FA;
    border: 1px solid #F2C9E6;
    color: #6b2155;
    font-size: 14px;
    line-height: 1.5;
    text-align: left;
  }
  .persona-label.placeholder {
    color: #aa7a98;
    font-style: italic;
    text-align: center;
  }
  .persona-hint {
    font-size: 12px;
    color: #aa7a98;
    margin-top: 4px;
  }
</style>

<div class="persona-wrap">
  <div class="persona-emoji">\U0001F9D8\U0001F3FC\u200D\u2640\uFE0F</div>
  <div class="hotspot" id="zone-hair"   title="Hair"></div>
  <div class="hotspot" id="zone-face"   title="Face"></div>
  <div class="hotspot" id="zone-clothes" title="Outfit"></div>
  <div class="hotspot" id="zone-nails-l" title="Nails"></div>
  <div class="hotspot" id="zone-nails-r" title="Nails"></div>
  <div class="persona-label placeholder" id="persona-output">
    Click the hair, face, outfit, or hands to see the top trend for that category among 18\u201324 year olds.
  </div>
  <div class="persona-hint">Tip: hover to see which zone you are about to click</div>
</div>

<script>
(function() {
  var labels = {
    "zone-hair":    %s,
    "zone-face":    %s,
    "zone-clothes": %s,
    "zone-nails-l": %s,
    "zone-nails-r": %s
  };
  var hotspots = document.querySelectorAll(".hotspot");
  var output = document.getElementById("persona-output");

  hotspots.forEach(function(el) {
    el.addEventListener("click", function() {
      hotspots.forEach(function(h) { h.classList.remove("active"); });
      el.classList.add("active");
      output.classList.remove("placeholder");
      output.innerHTML = labels[el.id];
    });
  });
})();
</script>
',
   jsonlite::toJSON(label_hair), jsonlite::toJSON(label_face),
   jsonlite::toJSON(label_clothes), jsonlite::toJSON(label_nails),
   jsonlite::toJSON(label_nails)))

widget_html
```

To finish our story, let's try to visualize our Pinterest user.
I gave the data for this user persona to ChatGPT and asked it to create a mood board representing our typical user, including how she probably looked each season.

![Pinterest board for the user](images/ChatGPT Image Jun 20, 2026, 07_47_33 PM.png)



![How did she look like](images/ChatGPT Image Jun 20, 2026, 07_50_22 PM.png)

# So who is she? 
## conclusion drawn from the data analysis

Putting all of this together, our typical 18-24 year old Pinterest girl is starting to look like a real person rather than just numbers. She most likely lives in New York or LA, because that's where the biggest share of this audience is concentrated. Her 2025 was running on a pretty predictable rhythm: she leans into fashion and self-expression all summer, pulls back once school starts, and leans on makeup instead when her outfit options get more restricted. Her nails are the most important part of her look with the searches peaking around summer vacation and again around the holidays she favors almond nails as a go-to all year. She probably has a January birthday, given how strongly "birthday makeup" trended that month, and she spent the back half of the year drifting toward an edgy, dark aesthetic in fall and winter before circling back to something softer and more minimalist by spring.
 
What this project really shows, though, is less about just one girl and more about how trend data can tell a story if you let it. Search volume alone just tells you what's popular, but layering in the seasonal patterns, the LLM-enriched themes, and the geography turns a list of numbers into something closer to a year in someone's life. It also says something about how tightly trends are tied to context: school schedules, holidays, even what a single brand does in a single month (Fenty makeup) can shift what an entire demographic is searching for. 

```{r}
library(htmlwidgets)
saveWidget(keyword_grid, "outputs/keyword_grid_interactive.html", selfcontained = TRUE)
saveWidget(timeline_plot, "outputs/timeline_interactive.html", selfcontained = TRUE)
saveWidget(fig, "outputs/sankey_interactive.html", selfcontained = TRUE)
```
